abstract.text: This article describes the essential parts of KeyboardKit.

inlineContent.text: KeyboardKit extends Apple’s native keyboard APIs and provides you with a lot more functionality. It lets you mimic a native iOS keyboard and tweak its style and behavior, or create completely custom keyboards.
view that mimics the native iOS keyboard. It can be customized and styled to great extent, and lets you replace any key content or key view of any action.
unlocks a lot of essential Pro features. Information about Pro features can be found at the end of this article.
KeyboardKit uses namespaces to make the API surface smaller, by nesting types in logical groups.
KeyboardKit has other namespaces as well, for more specific capabilities, like
Namespaces will not contain protocols, nor important types that are meant to be exposed as top-level types. This includes essential types like
namespace contains a lot of essential, keyboard-related types and views. By typing
, Xcode will list all essential types within this namespace.
The namespace has a lot of essential types, like
The namespace also has a lot of view-related types, like
is the most essential type in the library. Just make your
inherit this class to get access to a bunch of additional capabilities and view lifecycle functions.

Other namespaces define other context types, like

KeyboardKit automatically creates instances of these classes and injects them into

that defines an App Group, the store will automatically be set up to sync data between your app and its keyboard extension.

KeyboardKit automatically creates an instance of
. You can replace it at any time, by implementing the procotol from scratch or by inheriting and customizing the standard behavior.
article describes how to use a
Various views have separate styles as well, that can be applied with specific view modifiers.
KeyboardKit extends native types with keyboard-specific functionality like autocomplete, text analysis, etc. Check the extension section in the bottom of the documentation root for more info.

that mimics a native iOS keyboard. It can be used for alphabetic, numeric & symbolic keyboards, supports all supported locales, layouts, callouts, etc., and can be styled to great extent with styles & themes:
The view can be customized to great extent. You can pass in custom services & state, and replace any button or entire sub-component:

code: controller: controller,             // You can pass in a controller, or specific states and services
buttonContent: { $0.view },         // You can customize the content view of any button
buttonView: { $0.view },            // You can customize the entire view of any button
emojiKeyboard: { $0.view },         // You can customize the emoji keyboard, if any
toolbar: { params in params.view }  // You can customize the toolbar above the keyboard

inlineContent.text: to use the standard view, or return a custom view for any part of the keyboard. Each view builder provides you with view-related parameters with contextual information.
namespace has a lot of views, styles, and view-related types:
that mimics a native iOS keyboard and that can be customized to great extent. Read more above.
namespace has a set of keyboard
views and styles that can be used to mimic all parts of a native keyboard, as well as their gestures. A
renders the full button, while other views like
(not shown here) renders parts of it.
that just render a bottom keyboard row, with the same behavior as a
that works as a native keyboard switcher. It switches to the next system keyboard on tap and shows a popover menu with all available keyboards on long press.
that mimics a native number pad keyboard. The
automatically adds a number pad above itself when the
view that applies a minimum height to its content. KeyboardKit will by default always add a toolbar to the
to avoid callouts from being cut off, since custom keyboards can’t render outside of the frame.
Most views have a corresponding style, which can be used to style it, much like SwiftUI’s
view modifier, that can be applied to an individual button, or an entire view hierarchy.
that can be applied with a
concept instead, since the keyboard view is so complex. You can however apply explicit styles within the
capabilities, including full support for all
-related views and utilities, that make it easier to create even more powerful keyboards:
KeyboardKit Pro unlocks localized services for all supported
s, which lets you create fully localized keyboard views that automatically shows the correct buttons, autocomplete suggestions, and callout actions.
The various license tiers unlock different amount of
s, where Gold unlocks all. Read more in the
when a valid license is registered.
The keyboard has support for categories, runtime version adjustments and skintones. Read more about this in the
KeyboardKit Pro will automatically add an additional input toolbar above
on large iPad devices, which makes it easy to insert numeric values without switching keyboard type.
modifier to the keyboard view to control whether to show or hide the toolbar on all device types, or to customize the buttons on the input keyboard.
can be used to toggle between two toolbars, e.g. to place a main menu “behind” the autocomplete toolbar:

These previews can be used in the main application, to show users how the keyboard will look for different settings, styles, themes, etc.
